name: Half-floor layout
description: Floor-layout strategy in which an upper level is built over only a fraction of the level beneath, so part of the ground floor remains a single double-height volume open to the rafters while the other part carries a true second storey of low rooms. The partial upper plate becomes a mezzanine or sleeping loft overlooking the great hall, kitchen or living area below, reached by a stair or ladder set against the dividing line and guarded by a railing along the open edge. This is the canonical plan of barn conversions, rustic cabins with sleeping lofts, fantasy great halls with minstrel galleries, and modern houses that trade square footage for cathedral ceilings and a sense of vertical generosity.
placement_rules: Cover between 40% and 60% of the ground-floor footprint with the upper plate; below 40% the loft is too cramped to use, above 60% the double-height effect is lost and the building reads as a normal two-storey.

Keep the double-height portion contiguous and aligned with the building's main living function (great hall, living area, kitchen-dining) so the tall volume serves the most-used room rather than a leftover corner.

Run the dividing line between the floored and open halves parallel to the long axis or across the short axis in one clean cut — never an L-shaped or zig-zag mezzanine, which fragments the open volume.
Hold the open volume at 4 or 5 blocks of clear height so the cathedral effect reads; 3 blocks collapses back into a normal room with a low ceiling above it.
Place the stair or ladder against the wall that supports the upper plate so its top landing arrives directly on the loft floor without a separate corridor.
Line the entire open edge of the upper plate with a railing (fence, wall 1 block tall, or balustrade) — the overlook is the defining feature and also the main fall hazard.
Reserve the loft for low-ceiling-tolerant rooms (bedroom, study, storage); kitchens and bathrooms with their plumbing belong on the floored ground level.
